description: A curve to the right ahead. Reduce speed before entering the curve. Used for moderate curves (lower curvature-deflection product).
Dangerous sharp curve to the right ahead. Reduce speed significantly before entering. The sharp right-angle arrow distinguishes this from the gentler SP-6 curve sign.
Two consecutive curves in opposite directions ahead, starting to the right (S-curve). Reduce speed and be prepared for the road to curve right then left.

nameEs: Curva inversa pronunciada a la derecha
description: Two sharp consecutive curves in opposite directions ahead, starting to the right. More severe than SP-8 (gentle reverse curves). The sharp right-angle zigzag arrow distinguishes this from the smoother S-curve of SP-8.
Winding road ahead with multiple curves. Reduce speed and stay alert. The snaking arrow indicates three or more consecutive curves, unlike SP-8/SP-9 which show only two.
Intersection ahead where another road crosses your path. Be alert for cross traffic.
Side road entering from the right ahead. Watch for traffic merging or turning from the right side.
A road joins from the right ahead. Watch for merging traffic. Also called 'Entronque lateral' on some exams.
A road branches off at an angle from the right ahead. Watch for traffic merging or exiting at the oblique intersection.
Y-junction ahead where a lower priority road branches to the right. The road splits into two; choose your direction and signal early.
Road narrows equally on both sides ahead. Reduce speed and stay centered.
Road narrows on one side only — check the sign pictogram to see which side. Reduce speed and adjust lane position. Exam questions often ask which side is narrowing.
Speed bump (tope) ahead. Slow down to avoid vehicle damage. Extremely common throughout Mexico.
Steep downhill grade ahead. Use lower gear and avoid riding the brakes.
Railroad tracks cross the road ahead. Slow down, look both ways, and listen for trains.
Bridge ahead is narrower than the road. Slow down and watch for oncoming traffic.
School zone ahead. Reduce speed and watch for children crossing.
Danger of falling rocks ahead. Drive cautiously and watch for rocks or debris on the roadway. Common on mountain highways and roads cut through hillsides.
School zone ahead. Reduce speed and watch for children crossing. Fluorescent yellow-green pentagon shape with silhouettes of children carrying backpacks. Per 2023 SICT manual.
Road surface may be slippery, especially when wet. Reduce speed and avoid sudden maneuvers.
Two-way traffic ahead. Stay in your lane and watch for oncoming vehicles.
Traffic signal (semáforo) ahead. Be prepared to stop. Yellow diamond with traffic light symbol alerts drivers approaching a signalized intersection.
Roundabout (glorieta) ahead. Yield to traffic already in the circle. Very common in Mexican cities.
Traffic signal ahead. Prepare to stop if the light is red or turning yellow.
Pedestrian crossing ahead. Yield to pedestrians and be prepared to stop.
Wildlife crossing area. Watch for deer and other animals on or near the roadway, especially at dawn and dusk.
Road construction or maintenance work ahead. Reduce speed and watch for workers, equipment, and lane changes. Orange diamond distinguishes construction warnings from yellow preventive signs.
Come to a complete stop. Check for traffic and pedestrians before proceeding.
You must yield the right of way. Slow down and let other traffic pass before proceeding.
Maximum speed 30 km/h. Common in school zones and residential areas.
Maximum speed 40 km/h. Common in urban areas and near intersections.
Maximum speed 60 km/h. Standard urban speed limit in most Mexican cities.
Maximum speed 80 km/h. Common on periférico (ring roads) and secondary highways.
Maximum speed 110 km/h. Typical for autopistas (toll highways).
Traffic flows in one direction only. Do not drive against the flow.
Bridge, overpass, or tunnel with limited vertical clearance. Tall vehicles must check height.
You must turn left at this location. No other direction of travel is permitted.
Road or passage has a maximum width limit. Wide vehicles must check clearance.
Two-way traffic ahead. Stay in your lane and watch for oncoming vehicles. This is the restrictive (red circle) version of the warning sign.
Restricted free width ahead. Indicates the maximum allowed width (in meters) for vehicles passing through a narrow area where space is too limited for two vehicles to pass simultaneously.
Maximum weight limit. Indicates the maximum gross vehicle weight (in metric tons) allowed on this road or structure. Vehicles exceeding the posted weight are prohibited.
No stopping allowed. Both parking and temporary stopping are prohibited on this roadway. Red circle with an X cross.
Parking is prohibited in this area. Brief stops for loading/unloading may still be allowed. On exams, may also appear as 'No estacionarse.'
No stopping at all — not even briefly for passengers (no estacionarse ni detenerse). Stricter than no parking.
U-turns are prohibited. Drivers must not reverse direction at this location. Red circle with diagonal slash over a U-turn arrow.
U-turns are not allowed at this location. Find an alternative route to reverse direction.

nameEs: Prohibido el paso de maquinaria agrícola
description: Tractors, combines, and other farm equipment cannot use this road.
All vehicle occupants must wear seatbelts. Required by law throughout Mexico. Designated SR-34 in the 2023 SICT manual.
You must continue straight. No turns allowed at this intersection.
Indicates the direction of traffic circulation. Double-headed white arrow on a black rectangular sign showing traffic flows in both directions on the cross street.

Maximum allowed vehicle length. Indicates the maximum length (in meters) permitted for vehicles, including trucks and trailers, on this road or structure. Shows a truck silhouette with the width measurement below.
Driver extending left arm out the window, bent upward at elbow — signals slow down or stop to drivers behind.

nameEs: Señal de vuelta a la izquierda
description: Driver extending left arm straight out the window horizontally — signals a left turn to drivers behind. On exams, distinguish from the right turn signal: horizontal arm = left, L-shaped arm (bent upward) = right.

nameEs: Señal de vuelta a la derecha
description: Driver extending left arm out the window, bent upward at elbow (L-shape) — signals a right turn to drivers behind. On exams, distinguish from the left turn signal: L-shaped arm (bent upward) = right, horizontal arm = left.
Highway exit or road junction ahead. Prepare to change lanes if taking the exit.
Shows the available clearance width of a road, bridge, or tunnel for vehicles. Also called 'Anchura libre' — exams may use either term.
